findings.title: Greg-approval decision rule depends on 'unambiguous approval token' that is not enumerated
findings.claim: The criterion says Greg must use 'approved / send it / equivalent unambiguous OK', but 'equivalent' is judgment and the rule claims to resolve on input alone. Two agents seeing the same Greg message could reach different branches.
findings.evidence: 'Criterion: Greg explicitly says approved / send it / equivalent unambiguous OK.' — the word 'equivalent' breaks input-alone testability.
findings.why_it_matters: Greg-approval is the gatekeeper to send. An ambiguous criterion here is exactly the chaos the spec exists to fix; it leaks the old back-and-forth pattern into the new design.

findings.title: Send-window edge case at day 44 with partial Greg approval is referenced but never specified
findings.location: ## Decision Rules → Send-window check, edge case handling
findings.claim: Edge case says 'day 44 with partial Greg approval — escalate to Mike for go/no-go' but the procedure step pre_send_checklist also has its own escalate path; the relationship between gate_greg_approval (rounds, silence) and gate_pre_send (window) is not coordinated for the case where Greg has approved partially AND the window is closing.
findings.evidence: Decision Rules → Send-window check, Edge case handling. Procedure step 5 successors do not reference the day-44 case at all.
findings.why_it_matters: Late-quarter scrambles are the realistic failure mode of this process; the spec under-specifies the worst case it claims to handle.

findings.title: comments_accepted_pct may be a vanity metric — high % does not necessarily mean better letter
findings.location: ## Metrics Map → Agent Performance Metrics, greg_review_loop additional metric
findings.claim: Accepting all of Greg's comments reads as compliance, not quality. A high comments_accepted_pct could indicate Mike-as-author surrendered judgment, not that the letter is better.
findings.evidence: 'comments_accepted_pct | % of Greg's comments incorporated into the next revision' — no coupling to whether acceptance correlated with letter quality.
findings.why_it_matters: The Metrics Review Plan references this metric as a quality signal in trigger conditions implicitly. If it's measuring compliance, optimizing on it produces worse letters over time.

findings.title: Edge case for late-stage financial-model amendment after fact-check passes is missing
findings.claim: The 'Numbers updated mid-draft' edge case covers the gap between collect_inputs and fact_check, but not the gap between fact_check passing and send. If the financial model is amended after Greg approves but before send (e.g., a correction lands), the letter ships with stale numbers and no re-check is triggered.
findings.evidence: Edge Cases table covers 'Numbers updated mid-draft' but the trigger is 'between collect_inputs and fact_check'. Procedure step 7 (send) has no model-freshness check.
findings.why_it_matters: This is the exact failure the user described — numbers chaos. The spec catches one window of the problem and leaves the other open.

findings.title: Operating-highlights validation requires '≥3 highlights' as a hard gate but no edge case for legitimately quiet quarters
findings.claim: Some quarters legitimately have fewer than 3 newsworthy highlights. The validation rule will route every such quarter to escalate_to_mike, framing a normal state as a failure.
findings.evidence: 'Validation: ≥3 highlights' has no boundary edge case for sparse quarters.
findings.why_it_matters: Recurring escalation on quiet quarters trains Mike to ignore escalations or to pad highlights. Both degrade the process.

findings.title: Internal contradiction: success criterion says ≤2 edit rounds; greg_review_loop describes loop until edit_rounds reaches 2
findings.location: ## Output → Success criterion AND ## Procedure → step 5 (greg_review_loop), AND ## Decision Rules → Greg approves
findings.claim: Success says 'no more than 2 rounds of edits' (2 allowed). Procedure step 5 says loop until 'edit_rounds reaches 2 OR Greg unresponsive >7 days', and the gate_greg_approval branch says '|edit_rounds >= 2 or silent > 7d| escalate_to_mike'. The latter routes the second round to escalation rather than allowing it. So a process that has exactly 2 rounds — the success limit — never converges.
findings.evidence: Output success criterion vs. diagram edge label '|edit_rounds >= 2 or silent > 7d| escalate_to_mike'.
findings.why_it_matters: The diagram and the success criterion disagree on the boundary. The agent will escalate when the user's stated success criterion was met.

findings.title: pre_send_checklist 'Mike final approval' is a hidden human gate not represented as a Gate row
findings.claim: gate_pre_send is listed with method=agent. But its verification list includes 'Mike final approval' — that's a human gate, not an agent check. The gate's stated method is wrong for one of its sub-checks.
findings.evidence: Gates table: gate_pre_send method='agent'. Procedure step 6 inputs include 'Mike final approval'. Build Notes 'Out of scope: do NOT auto-send without Mike's final approval'.
findings.why_it_matters: If the build agent reads the gate as agent-verifiable, it may auto-clear it; the safety property (no Mike-bypass send) becomes implicit instead of enforced.

findings.title: The escalate_to_mike step has a non-deterministic successor ('back to the step Mike directs') with no enumeration of legal targets
findings.claim: Successor 'if Mike resumes the run: → (back to the step Mike directs)' is open-ended; the spec does not list which steps are legal resume targets. A build agent honoring the spec literally cannot validate Mike's instruction.
findings.why_it_matters: Without a legal-target list, Mike could resume into a logically illegal state (e.g., resume at send without re-running fact-check after a model change). The fact-check protection becomes circumventable through escalation.
